Q1 — Place: The vegetable market behind my mother's house at 6am, before the crowd. The light was that thin pale color, and the vendors were still unpacking. I remember the smell of coriander hitting the wet stone floor where they'd just hosed it down, and the way the green chilies sat in piles next to the heap of bitter gourd, and how each pile of leaves had its own kind of damp wilt. I'd walk slowly between the stalls just smelling things and pinching the lemons to see which ones felt heaviest for their size.
Q2 — Habit: I rearrange spice cabinets in other people's houses. If I'm staying somewhere overnight, I'll quietly reorganize their cumin and turmeric while making chai. I also do this thing where, when I'm eating something interesting in a restaurant, I'll close my eyes and try to mentally separate the dish into its parts — okay that's caramelized onion, that's a fenugreek seed, that's a roasted not boiled tomato. People who know me well will hand me a forkful and just say 'guess.' Half the time I get it within three ingredients.
Q3 — Explanation: I'll explain why a dal tastes different the next day. People think it's just absorption — the lentils soaking up the spice. What's actually happening: the fat globules from the tadka have had time to redistribute around individual lentil particles, the starch has continued to break down slightly into smaller sugar molecules which taste sweeter, and the volatile compounds in the spices have re-bonded with the proteins. The dal becomes more itself overnight. It's chemistry, not magic. But it's chemistry that only happens if you didn't put it in the fridge too quickly and let the heat finish its work.
Q4 — Conflict: I broke up a friendship at 24 over a dinner my friend made. She'd served us a beautifully presented chicken curry that was, technically, perfectly executed — but every spice in it had been added at the wrong moment. The cumin tasted raw because it hadn't bloomed first; the garam masala had been added at the start so all the top notes had burned off. I tried to be polite but she could see my face. She accused me of being a snob. The real story was that she thought cooking was just following recipes; I knew it was timing. I couldn't pretend her dish was good. She never forgave me.
Q5 — Sound: The exact pitch of mustard seeds popping in hot oil. Not when they're popping — the half second BEFORE they pop, when the oil starts ticking against them, like a tiny rain. That's the moment you have to throw in the next ingredient. Two seconds late and the seeds burn; one second early and the next spice doesn't release. I learned that sound before I learned to read recipes. My hands know what to do when I hear it.
Q6 — Body: I was helping a friend cook and she handed me a tomato to chop. The second it was in my palm, my hand pulled back and I asked her where she'd gotten it. The weight was wrong — too light for its size, hollow inside. I knew before I cut it that it would be mealy and watery. She'd bought it from a different shop. When I cut it open, the inside was exactly what my hand had predicted. We both stood there looking at the bad tomato. She'd handled it for ten minutes and noticed nothing.
Q7 — Unworded: The feeling of a meal being COMPLETE — not just delicious, but balanced. There's a moment in a perfectly composed plate where the acid and the heat and the sweet and the fat all sit in proportion, and your body recognizes it the way it recognizes a song that resolves to its tonic. You're not just full. You're settled. Most meals don't have it. When one does, I can taste exactly which ingredient is doing the work to hold the others up. I've never found words for that recognition. It's a kind of mathematics you eat.
